<Summary source="https://www.robert-schuman.eu/en/monitor/1933-slovenian-general-elections-on-24-april-pm-janez-jansa-could-come-out-ahead-but-not-be-able-to-form-a-majority-to-govern">
## Summary

**Disclaimer:** This article appears to be from April 2022, covering the lead-up to the **April 24, 2022 Slovenian parliamentary election** — not the 2026 election referenced in the forecasting question. Its relevance to the 2026 election is therefore limited, though it provides useful historical and political context.

---

### Key Facts

- **Election date covered:** April 24, 2022 (90 seats in the Državni Zbor)
- **Pre-election polling (Mediana Institute, March 21-24, 2022):** SDS polling at **16.8%**, GS (Freedom Movement) at **16.7%** — essentially tied
- **20 political parties** were competing in the 2022 election

### Political Context
- **Janez Janša (SDS)** was the incumbent PM, campaigning on higher wages/pensions and a drop in unemployment (4.1% in February 2022), but faced significant public protests and EU criticism over media policy
- **Robert Golob (GS)** was a new political entrant, a former head of Slovenia's electricity distribution company, running on green transition, welfare modernization, and rule of law
- Analysts expected the opposition (GS + left-wing KUL coalition) to perform well
- SDS faced difficulty forming a majority even if it came first, as its potential coalition partners were struggling to clear parliamentary thresholds

### Opposition Coalition (KUL)
- Comprised Social Democrats (SD), Marjan Sarec List (LMS), Alenka Bratušek Party (SAB), and The Left (L)
- Agreed that the PM post would go to the leader of the party with the most votes among them

*This article does not contain data directly relevant to the **2026** Slovenian parliamentary election.*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.coface.com/news-economy-and-insights/business-risk-dashboard/country-risk-files/slovenia">
## Summary: Coface Slovenia Country File

**Note:** This article appears to be from sometime in 2025, as it references elections "expected to take place as scheduled in April 2026" (the actual election was scheduled for March 22, 2026), suggesting the data may be slightly dated.

---

### Political Situation (Most Relevant to Question)

- The **Freedom Movement (GS)**, led by Robert Golob, won the April 2022 elections with **41 of 90 seats**, forming a left-wing coalition with Social Democrats (7 seats) and the Left Party (5 seats).
- The **Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS)** holds **27 seats** in opposition, alongside New Slovenia (Christian-democratic, 8 seats).
- SDS called for a snap election in 2025 due to "mounting disagreements and controversies in the government," though elections were expected as scheduled.
- The governing coalition faces challenges around **stalled healthcare and pension reforms**, a lack of national consensus, and ongoing social issues (gender pay gap, rising poverty).

### Economic Context

- Slovenia's economy is growing, supported by private consumption, wage reforms, and EU recovery funds.
- Key vulnerabilities include dependence on German automotive industry and imported energy.
- Budget deficit is shrinking; public debt declining after peaking above 80% of GDP in 2020.
- Current account surplus expected to continue in 2025.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.robert-schuman.eu/en/monitor/6746-janez-jansa-s-sds-rises-in-the-polls-is-robert-golob-and-his-svoboda-movement-s-time-counted-in-the-slovenian-general-election-on-22-march">
## Summary of Robert Schuman Foundation Article on the 2026 Slovenian Parliamentary Election

**Note:** The article appears to be cut off before completion, so some sections may be incomplete.

### Electoral Context & Background
- Elections scheduled for **March 22, 2026**, called by President Nataša Pirc Musar on January 6
- 90 seats in the lower house (Državni Zbor) to be filled
- In 2022, GS (Freedom Movement) won a **landslide victory with 41 seats**; SDS won **27 seats**

### Current Government's Weaknesses (GS/Golob)
- Multiple **cabinet scandals and reshuffles** between 2023–2025, creating an impression of instability
- The **"Litijska affair"** (December 2022): Golob accused of corruption and improper use of influence regarding judiciary building purchases
- Interior Minister resignation (2022) amid accusations of interference in police affairs
- Controversial **Roma-related policing law** (October 2025) drew criticism from both the far right and international human rights bodies
- These successive scandals have **eroded GS's initial polling advantage**

### SDS/Janša's Rising Strength
- SDS popularity has been **rising since the 2024 European elections**
- Janša running for PM for a **fourth time**, campaigning on conservative values, anti-immigration stance, and press criticism
- Described as aligning with Orbán, Fico, and Babiš in populist style, though notably **pro-Ukraine and pro-NATO**
- Received endorsement from **EPP President Manfred Weber** in early February 2026

### Polling Situation
- The article notes the **outgoing government's decline in popularity** in polls, but does not provide specific polling numbers or seat projections

### Electoral System Notes
- 4% threshold required for parliamentary representation
- Proportional representation with preferential voting across 8 constituencies
- Two guaranteed minority seats (Italian and Hungarian communities)
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.foederalist.eu/2026/01/ep-seat-projection-january-2026.html">
## Summary

**Disclaimer:** This article is about European Parliament seat projections as of January 2026, and contains **no information directly relevant** to the 2026 Slovenian parliamentary election or the relative seat counts of SDS and GS. It does not mention either party or Slovenia's domestic election.

---

### Article Content (European Parliament Projection, January 2026)

The article presents updated EP seat projections comparing November 2025 and January 2026 figures:

**Key group standings (baseline scenario, Jan. 2026):**
- EPP: 177 seats (+2 vs. November) — best figure since July 2025
- S&D: 122 seats (–2) — historic low; EPP lead over S&D now exceeds 50 seats
- RE: 71 seats (unchanged) — prolonged multi-year downward trend
- Greens/EFA: 37 seats (unchanged)
- Left: 53 seats (+2) — gaining partly at S&D's expense
- ECR: 80 seats (–1)
- Patriots for Europe (PfE): 104 seats (–1)
- ESN: 38 seats (–1)

**Notable observation:** For the first time since August 2022, all three far-right groups simultaneously lost seats compared to the prior projection — though the article cautions this may reflect normal statistical fluctuation rather than a definitive trend reversal.
</Summary>

**PiS to Announce Prime Minister Candidate at March 7 Convention in Kraków**
Jarosław Kaczyński, President of the Law and Justice (PiS) party, announced the start of an electoral 'march' beginning on March 7, 2026, according to a Saturday (February 28) statement. Zbigniew Kuźmiuk, speaking on Monday (March 2), clarified that the March 7 date marks a party convention to be held at the 'Sokół' hall in Kraków, where PiS will present its candidate for Prime Minister. Kuźmiuk stated that while he does not know the candidate's name, the party's election program must be ready by May and will 'have a face,' which will be the Prime Minister candidate. Although Kaczyński reportedly made his choice, unofficial reports suggest he is still considering candidates, with names mentioned including Zbigniew Bogucki, Tobiasz Bocheński, Przemysław Czarnek, and Anna Krupka, as well as several local officials. The convention is expected to launch the campaign for the next parliamentary elections. The article also notes a separate report that President Volodymyr Zelenskyy offered something following an Iranian retaliation and that the Polish government decided not to send planes to evacuate Polish citizens, a decision evaluated by a diplomat.
Original language: pl
Publish date: March 02, 2026 11:16 AM
Source:[FAKT24.pl](https://www.fakt.pl/polityka/pis-zapowiada-konwencje-ujawni-nazwisko-kandydata-na-premiera/146z6gy)

**Journalist Calls for Human Connection Over Conspiracy Theories at Gwanghwamun 'Yoon Again' Rallies**
On March 2, 2026, an article published in OhmyNews by an unnamed journalist analyzes recent gatherings at Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul, South Korea. The report details how groups such as the 'National Movement for Straightening Korea' (led by Pastor Jeon Kwang-hoon, who was recently indicted for inciting a riot at the Western District Court) have held rallies demanding the release of President Yoon Suk-yeol and Pastor Jeon, while promoting 'Yoon Again' (re-election) and unfounded election fraud conspiracy theories. 

The journalist observed that other groups, including 'Shin-Yu-Yeon-dae' and 'Free University,' also focused on the 'Yoon Again' slogan. 'Shin-Yu-Yeon-dae' reportedly used hateful slogans like 'China Out' to stir anti-Chinese sentiment and link it to election fraud, while 'Free University' featured young people waving large Taegukgi flags and shouting 'Youth must wake up.' These participants echoed claims made by conspiracy theorist Jeon Han-gil and Im Joon-suk (leader of the Reformation Party) during a debate the previous day, asserting that 'evidence is overwhelming,' a 'giant cartel' exists, and the Election Commission's servers must be inspected.

The author cites social psychologist Erich Fromm's book <Escape from Freedom>, noting that feelings of loneliness and social isolation can drive individuals toward authoritarianism and fascism. Fromm argues that even baseless beliefs like religion or nationalism can serve as a refuge from the fear of being alone. The article concludes with a hope that participants at the Gwanghwamun Square rallies, currently illuminated by the electronic sign displaying poet Park So-ran's line 'I wonder what you eat and how you get by,' might find solace in human connection rather than conspiracy theories, potentially breaking their ties with extreme right-wing authoritarianism.

Source:[오마이뉴스](https://www.ohmynews.com/NWS_Web/View/at_pg.aspx)

**Japan's Shortest Campaign: Populism, Scandal, and the Silence of Democracy**
On January 23, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, who became Japan's first female Prime Minister with popular support on October 25, 2025, dissolved the House of Representatives, setting the next election for February 8. This decision triggered the shortest post-war campaign period in Japanese history, lasting only 16 days, with voters facing a choice between a center-left stance and a rightward shift toward nationalist economic stimulus and a hardline policy against China. Takaichi's coalition, comprising the dominant Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) and the newly established Japan Innovation Party, aims to secure a majority in the 465-seat chamber to advance its agenda, though critics fear her rhetoric blaming immigrants, tourists, and China for economic woes could fuel inflation and xenophobia. The author, a Chinese-Japanese descendant, argues that the cultural norm of 'reading the air' (空気を読む), which prioritizes harmony over debate, has created a vacuum filled by populist rhetoric and political apathy. This environment has allowed the far-right Sanseitō party, led by Sohei Kamiya, to gain traction with slogans like 'Japanese first' (a nod to Donald Trump's 'America First'), while a subculture known as 'Sana-katsu' has emerged among young women who idolize Takaichi as a pop star. The article highlights a critical lack of public deliberation, noting that Japanese schools prioritize rote memorization over debate training, leading voters to treat politics as entertainment. Furthermore, the text details systemic links between the LDP and the Unification Church, founded by Sun Myung Moon in 1954; according to the South Korean newspaper Hankyoreh, the church financed at least 295 LDP candidates in the October 2021 lower house election. Following a 2022 criminal investigation into the assassination of former Prime Minister Shinzō Abe, which revealed his family's collusion with the church, a Tokyo court ordered the dissolution of two related organizations in March 2025 for manipulating followers into financial sacrifices. Despite these scandals, several legislators benefiting from the cult remain in power. Former Foreign Minister Makiko Tanaka suggested to Nippon TV on January 22 that the sudden dissolution was a strategic distraction to hide corruption ties, stating, 'Political and money issues, the relationship between the Unification Church and the LDP... to hide all this, dissolution is the fastest way.' The author observes that the election has been reduced to a spectacle, with viral content like an anime-edited campaign video garnering over 100,000 views in a day, while students complain about campaign noise interfering with university entrance exams. The author, who does not have voting rights in Japan, expresses concern that citizens are surrendering their will to 'idols' without understanding the consequences, as evidenced by a campaign flyer reading 'Protect the lives of Japanese people' distributed to the author without knowledge of their Chinese ancestry.
Original language: es
Publish date: March 02, 2026 06:00 AM
Source:[Global Voices en Español](https://es.globalvoices.org/2026/03/02/por-que-las-proximas-elecciones-de-la-camara-baja-de-japon-se-sienten-como-una-carrera-hacia-el-fondo/)

**Last Week: Fico signals he is ready to interfere with the electoral system - The Slovak Spectator**
In Slovakia, Prime Minister Robert Fico and his ruling party, Smer, are preparing an amendment to the electoral system that would ban mail-in voting, a move described as a 'worrying intervention' inspired by US rhetoric. On Wednesday, Fico claimed no plans to change voting rules but admitted coalition MPs are discussing it; his chief advisor, MEP Erik Kaliňák, subsequently stated a draft amendment was 'ready' on the disinformation channel Infovojna. Just one day later, Smer deputy speaker MP Tibor Gašpar confirmed the amendment would be submitted to parliament at its April session. The opposition party Progressive Slovakia (PS) raised concerns early in the week, while the junior coalition partner Hlas expressed skepticism but is noted for a track record of eventually supporting such changes. This strategy mirrors comments made by US President Donald Trump in August, who pledged on Truth Social to 'FIGHT LIKE HELL TO BRING HONESTY AND INTEGRITY BACK TO OUR ELECTIONS' regarding mail-in ballots. The article also notes other recent developments in Slovakia: Samsung announced it will end production in western Slovakia, affecting 800 blue-collar workers, and the government is retaliating against Ukraine over electricity for oil.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 01, 2026 08:26 PM
Source:[spectator.sme.sk](https://spectator.sme.sk/opinion/c/last-week-fico-signals-he-is-ready-to-interfere-with-the-electoral-system)

**Slovenian Activist Nika Kovac Secures EU Win for 'My Voice, My Choice' Initiative**
At 33 years old, Slovenian activist Nika Kovac successfully pressured the European Commission to accept her 'My Voice, My Choice' citizens' initiative, which gathered 1.2 million signatures. Kovac, who began her activism at age 22 with a national campaign and founded the '8 March' institute, is credited with securing approximately 10 legislative changes in Slovenia, particularly regarding consent. Her drive intensified after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that states could ban abortion, prompting her to focus on preventing similar setbacks in Europe. She noted the high stakes of her campaign, stating, 'If we had lost, we would have been seen as a bunch of crazies.'

**PSD Internal Election in Braga Exposes Party Divisions Amidst Pending Corruption Charges**
In the recent internal elections of the Portuguese Social Democratic Party (PSD), Carlos Eduardo Reis defeated the incumbent president Paulo Cunha to lead the Braga district, a significant political stronghold. According to the article, Reis secured victory with the backing of Barcelos, Vila Verde, Amares, and Terras de Bouro, while Cunha won in Vila Nova de Famalicão (945 against 602 votes) but lost in Braga and Guimarães. Reis, a deputy who suspended his parliamentary mandate in February 2025, is currently facing pending accusations from the Ministry of Public Prosecution regarding the 'Tutti Frutti' operation, which investigates alleged favoritism since 2018 involving public contracts and includes charges of active corruption, prevarication, and trafficking of influence. A source from the national direction told the Observador that having a local leader with such pending accusations 'is not good for the party,' describing the situation as 'extraordinarily delicate' given Braga's political influence. The national leadership, previously aligned with Rui Rio and Hugo Soares, had previously withdrawn Reis's political confidence after he was accused of approximately two dozen crimes. Reis defended his candidacy to Lusa, stating he is not running 'against the Government' but rather to promote 'political intervention' and 'renewal of the district's cadres,' noting Cunha has held the post for three terms. However, the internal conflict is viewed as a proxy for the broader power struggle between supporters of party leader Luís Montenegro and the national direction. In response to the 'Tutti Frutti' scandal, the PSD adopted a new ethical regulation to be voted on by the National Council, mandating that members uphold 'the primacy of public interest' and 'integrity and transparency.' Reis, whose father Fernando Reis was also a prominent PSD figure, emphasized that his candidacy reflects a 'different opinion' rather than a contest of sympathy for the Government, though the article notes Cunha's campaign explicitly highlighted Montenegro's support.
Original language: pt
Publish date: March 01, 2026 03:26 PM

**Charente Libre Readers Association Hosts Debate on Municipal Election Coverage**
Charente Libre (CL) and the readers' association 'CL et Nous' are hosting a debate titled 'Municipales : CL met le paquet' on Saturday, February 28 at 10:30 AM at Bar à son in La Couronne. Ahead of the municipal elections scheduled for March 15 and March 22, 2026, the newspaper has committed to a dense and multifaceted coverage strategy including in-depth articles, investigations, reports, and digital formats, alongside a special weekly broadcast 'CLes municipales' airing every Friday on charentelibre.fr where journalists discuss the ongoing campaign. During the event, moderated by Thierry Cordebeoeuf, president of the association, journalists Ismaël Karroum, deputy editor-in-chief, and Christelle Lasaires, deputy head of the Angoulême agency, will address reader questions regarding how to manage the proliferation of candidate lists, how to distinguish verified information from rumors, and how to respond to accusations of bias.

**Rachida Dati's Paris Campaign: Overcoming Scandals and Polls Point to Second Round Victory**
Rachida Dati, the ambitious Mayor of the 7th arrondissement of Paris and former Minister, has officially been relieved of her Culture portfolio to focus on her campaign for the Paris City Hall (Hôtel de Ville), despite a series of significant setbacks. These include the Louvre museum heist in October 2025, which highlighted her 'glorious management' issues, and judicial investigations involving perquisitions at her home, the Ministry of Culture, and the 7th arrondissement town hall in mid-December 2025 regarding suspected remuneration by GDF Suez during her time as a European deputy. Additionally, she faces a pending trial for corruption in the Carlos Ghosn affair scheduled for September 2026, which could force her to resign if convicted after winning the mayoralty. According to an Ifop poll for L'Opinion and Sud Radio published on February 22, 2026, three weeks before the first round on March 15, Dati is ranked second behind socialist candidate Emmanuel Grégoire. However, she is projected to win the second round in four out of five scenarios: a direct duel with Grégoire (53%), a narrow victory in a three-way race against Horizons candidate Pierre-Yves Bournazel (43%), and a three-way race against Sophia Chikirou and Emmanuel Grégoire. Dati's campaign has been criticized for a lack of substantive program impact, with her proposals to 'put beauty everywhere' and relax rent controls failing to mark public opinion, partly due to her refusal to debate opponents. On February 26, she told BFMTV she would be 'necessarily the target of other candidates' if she debated. Despite this, she has launched attacks against Pierre-Yves Bournazel, labeling his candidacy as a potential failure or a means to maintain the left, while former Prime Minister Édouard Philippe has called for a 'great gathering of the right and center' for the second round, potentially helping Dati eliminate Bournazel. Dati warns that any vote dispersion in the first round could lead to the re-election of the Grégoire-Hidalgo team, a scenario she deems 'worse'.

**Nepal's 2026 Election: Gen Z Movement Reshapes Democracy Amidst Anti-Establishment Wave**
According to an article published on March 1, 2026, by Online Khabar, Nepal is undergoing an extraordinary general election in 2026, described as a direct outcome of the 'Gen Z Movement' that erupted on Bhado 23 (August 2025). This movement, triggered by the shooting of youths aged 20-22 and subsequent government crackdowns, led to the collapse of the K.P. Oli-led government, which held nearly a two-thirds majority, and the dissolution of Parliament. An interim government led by Sushila Karki was formed, and the general election date was announced. The election atmosphere is intensified by a confrontation between former Kathmandu Mayor Balendra (Balen) Shah and Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli. The article highlights that the Gen Z Movement was not a 'temporary youthful impulse' but a rare social moment addressing corruption, transparency, socio-economic inequality, and freedom of expression. Social media has transformed from a mere propaganda tool into a powerful instrument influencing voter decisions, particularly in the Madhesh and rural (Mofasol) areas, where enthusiasm is highest. Voters are increasingly relying on their social media 'feeds' rather than traditional family or societal endorsements. The article draws parallels to anti-establishment waves globally, citing the 2014 Indian general election (Narendra Modi and BJP rise following the 2G and Coalgate scandals), the 2016 Lithuanian election (Lithuanian Peasant and Greens Union), the 2020 Slovakian election (Ordinary People and Independents), and the Kosovo Self-Determination Movement. These examples illustrate that when institutions lose moral legitimacy, corruption becomes systemic, and youth lose faith in the older generation, the rise of 'outsider' leadership becomes inevitable. While Balen Shah has not completed a full term as mayor, his administration established a precedent for 'work culture' and 'results with willpower,' distinguishing him from traditional parties. The election is viewed as a rare opportunity for Nepali democracy to redefine itself, pitting established parties that fear change against new political energy from the movement. The article suggests that traditional election standards, such as the impossibility of a majority in a mixed election system, may be broken due to high voter enthusiasm and the impact of social media information, which includes both correct and incorrect data.
Original language: hi
Publish date: March 01, 2026 04:35 AM
Source:[Online Khabar](https://www.onlinekhabar.com/2026/03/1880783/the-election-colors-are-spreading-from-clip-to-reel)

**Buzzers Used Sparingly: Five Impressions from the SWR 'Triell' of Baden-Württemberg's Top Candidates**
The SWR 'Triell' of Baden-Württemberg's top candidates for the state election, featuring CDU's Manuel Hagel, Greens' Cem Özdemir, and AfD's Markus Frohnmaier, aired live on February 25, 2026, with strong viewership and high expectations. The 90-minute debate, structured into three thematic blocks—economy, migration/safety, and housing/education/health—was marked by balanced participation and no clear winner. Hagel projected confidence and leadership, Özdemir emphasized calm and experience, while Frohnmaier adopted an aggressive tone, attacking both rivals and challenging the SWR moderators. A major controversy erupted after a viral 2018 video resurfaced, in which Hagel described a student with 'brown hair, deer-brown eyes,' sparking public backlash. During the debate, Frohnmaier directly confronted Hagel on the issue, prompting Hagel to express regret and clarify he would not use such language today. Thematic imbalance was evident: migration dominated the discussion (30 minutes), while education—key to state governance—received only about five minutes, focusing narrowly on a social media ban. The FDP unsuccessfully attempted to block the broadcast via legal action, while 'Stuttgart gegen rechts' protested the AfD's inclusion, though CDU supporters were the most visible at the event. A buzzer system was introduced to allow interruptions, but was used sparingly—only six times total, mostly by Frohnmaier—reflecting a deliberate restraint among candidates to maintain a composed, statesmanlike image. The event marked a pivotal moment in the final 12 days of the campaign.
Original language: de
Publish date: February 25, 2026 06:48 AM
Source:[SÜDKURIER Online](https://www.suedkurier.de/baden-wuerttemberg/fuenf-eindruecke-vom-swr-triell-der-landtags-spitzenkandidaten-113580632)

**New Survey: Slovenia Heading Toward Major Rightward Shift in Upcoming Elections**
Slowenien is heading toward a significant rightward shift in its upcoming parliamentary elections, according to a February 16, 2026, survey by Mediana for RTV Slovenija. The ruling liberal Freedom Movement (GS), led by Prime Minister Robert Golob, is projected to secure only 23 seats, falling short of a majority. The conservative Democratic Party of Slovenia (SDS), led by long-time opposition leader Janez Janša, is expected to win 30 seats, potentially forming a center-right coalition with other right-leaning parties, including the newly formed populist Resni.ca, which is projected to gain four seats. Resni.ca, founded by anti-COVID measures activist Zoran Stevanović, campaigns against the political establishment and the European Union with the slogan 'Dem Volk die Macht' and has gained traction by challenging both the SDS and the mainstream political order. Despite Janša’s efforts to consolidate the right wing with hardline positions on migration and EU skepticism—modeled after Hungary’s Viktor Orbán—he remains vulnerable to attacks from populist rivals due to his pro-Ukraine stance. Notably, Stevanović previously signed a document in October 2021 explicitly ruling out cooperation with SDS, raising concerns about potential political instability if Resni.ca achieves a surprise breakthrough. The survey shows the government coalition (GS, SD, and Left) projected at 39 seats, below the 45 needed for a majority.

Publish date: February 16, 2026 03:57 PM
Source:[www.kleinezeitung.at](https://www.kleinezeitung.at/kaernten/alpeadria/20594775/slowenien-steuert-bei-wahl-auf-massiven-rechtsruck-zu)

Slovenia is heading toward a significant rightward shift in its upcoming parliamentary elections, scheduled for one month from the publication date. The results of the new right-populist party Resni.ca (Truth), which adopts a radical anti-system stance, are being closely watched with anticipation.

**Slovenia: Rightward Shift Expected in Upcoming Parliamentary Elections**
Slovenia is heading toward a political shift to the right ahead of its upcoming parliamentary elections. The liberal Freedom Movement (GS), led by Prime Minister Robert Golob, is expected to fall behind the conservative Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS), led by long-time opposition leader Janez Janša, who has strong prospects of securing a center-right majority. The new right-populist party Resni.ca (Truth), founded by anti-system activist Zoran Stevanović, is also gaining attention, with polling showing it could win four seats. A survey by Mediana for public broadcaster RTV Slovenija projects the ruling coalition—GS, Social Democrats (SD), and Left—will secure only 39 seats, with GS winning 23. The SDS is projected to gain 30 seats, and with support from smaller right-wing parties, could reach exactly 45 seats. However, tensions exist between Janša and other right-wing factions, notably after former foreign minister Anže Logar founded the party 'Demokrati,' which is projected to win six seats. Janša, who has led the SDS since 1993 and served as prime minister three times (2004–2008, 2012–2013, 2020–2022), presents himself as a fighter against a post-communist establishment, despite past imprisonments for treason (1988) and corruption (2014). While Janša opposes the EU and adopts a hardline migration stance in the style of Hungary’s Viktor Orbán, he remains a strong supporter of Ukraine—making him vulnerable to criticism from other populist forces. Resni.ca’s leader Stevanović, who runs an active social media campaign under the slogan 'Power to the People,' has publicly rejected any cooperation with the SDS, citing a 2021 document in which he banned collaboration. Political observers suggest Resni.ca could deliver a surprise result, potentially threatening SDS’s dominance despite its projected lead.
Original language: de
Publish date: February 16, 2026 02:44 PM

**Slovenia’s SDS holds narrow lead in latest poll ahead of March general elections**
According to a new opinion poll conducted by Ninamedia for the weekly Mladina and published on February 1, 2026, Slovenia’s main opposition party, the Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS), led by former Prime Minister Janez Jansa, holds a narrow lead in the race ahead of the March general elections, with 22.6% of respondents indicating they would vote for it. The ruling Freedom Movement, led by Prime Minister Robert Golob, trails with 19.1% support. The rightwing bloc, anchored by SDS and including allied parties NSi, SLS, Fokus, and Anze Logar’s Democrats, holds a slight advantage over the centre-left coalition. The poll suggests this margin could be sufficient for Jansa to form a new government, though the outcome remains uncertain less than two months before election day. Support for other parties is relatively evenly distributed: 6.5% for the joint NSi–SLS–Fokus list, 6.4% for the Social Democrats (SD), and 6.1% each for the Levica–Vesna joint list and Anze Logar’s Democrats. The extra-parliamentary Resni.ca party has 3.5% support, close to the parliamentary threshold. The survey also revealed a high number of undecided voters—19.7%—some of whom may ultimately abstain. The poll was conducted between January 26 and 27, 2026, among 700 respondents.

**Centre-right SDS leads the Freedom Movement in Slovenia: survey - News**
According to a public opinion poll commissioned by the news portal Siol and reported by the Slovenian news agency STA, Slovenia's centre-right Democratic Party (SDS) leads with 22.5% support, a 1.4 percentage point decrease from December 2025. The Freedom Movement follows with 18.3% support, down 0.7 points. The Social Democrats (SD) gained one point to reach 8.7%, while Anže Logar's Democrats saw a 0.2-point decline to 7.3%. The coalition of the Left and the non-parliamentary green party Vesna increased nearly two points to 6.4%. The opposition coalition of New Slovenia (NSi), the non-parliamentary People's Party (SLS), and Focus polled at 6%, followed by the anti-establishment party Truth at 5.3% and MEP Vladimir Prebilič's Renaissance party at 3.1%. Slovenia is set to hold parliamentary elections on March 22, 2026.

**Slovenian Opposition Party Sds Holds Lead Over Ruling Party Ahead of Upcoming Elections**
In Slovenia, the largest opposition party, the Slovenian Democratic Party (Sds), maintains a lead over the ruling party of Prime Minister Robert Golob, according to a survey commissioned by the news portal Siol. The Sds received 22.5% of voter support, a decrease of 1.4 percentage points from December, while Golob's Freedom Movement (Liberals) garnered 18.3%, down 0.7 points. The Social Democrats (Sd), in government, saw a 1 percentage point increase to 8.7%, and Anže Logar's Democrats declined by 0.2 points to 7.3%. The joint list of the Left and the ecological party Vesna gained nearly 2 points, reaching 6.4%. The opposition coalition New Slovenia (NSi), Slovenian People's Party (Sls), and Focus secured 6%, followed by the anti-establishment party Resni.ca with 5.3% and the Eurodeputy Vladimir Prebilič's party Renaissance with 3.1%. Parliamentary elections in Slovenia are scheduled for March 22, 2026.

**Gašper Stražišar’s Investigative Articles on Slovenian Social and Political Issues in 2025–2026**
Gašper Stražišar, a Slovenian journalist, has authored multiple articles on diverse social, political, and institutional issues in Slovenia as of early 2026. Key topics include political discourse around the conservative party SDS’s stance on NGOs, with leader Janez Janša threatening to 'close the taps' on non-governmental organizations, which he labels as a 'political paramilitary left'. The article 'Priloga k zborniku razburila avtorje' highlights controversy over a publication by the Jasa association, deemed controversial and potentially criminal. Other articles cover education policy, such as the 2025 Higher Education Act that removes preferential admission for students with special needs, and concerns over the impact of the change on equity. The media sector is also under scrutiny, with reports on Russian-aligned bots overwhelming comments on MMC RTV Slovenia, and the 2026 budget for RTV Slovenija projecting minimal financial surplus. Additional articles address social justice issues, including calls for easing entry for Palestinian students, concerns about fire safety in youth homes after a January 2025 fire, and the long-standing legal limbo faced by refugee Mohamed Alzoubija. The author also examines public services, such as the underfunded trolleybus system, the lack of affordable support tools for schools, and the rise in public transport fares by 15%. The articles reflect a critical, investigative tone focused on systemic challenges in governance, media, education, and human rights, with consistent attribution to Stražišar as the author and publication dates ranging from January 2025 to January 2026.

**Slovenia Sets Stage for Pivotal Parliamentary Elections | Politics**
Slovenia is set to hold parliamentary elections on March 22, 2026, following a decree signed by President Natasa Pirc-Musar, marking a pivotal moment for the nation's democracy. The current center-left coalition government, led by Prime Minister Robert Golob and comprising the Freedom Movement, Social Democrats, and the Left, faces strong opposition from Janez Jansa’s Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS), a populist force still influential in Slovenian politics. President Pirc-Musar has emphasized the need for a swift formation of a new government, with the mandate to be awarded to the candidate securing 46 out of 90 parliamentary votes. She has also called on political leaders and the media to foster a calm, solution-oriented pre-election environment, avoiding divisive rhetoric. The outcome of the election is expected to be closely contested, shaping Slovenia’s political trajectory in the EU and NATO.

**Slovenia's President Sets Legislative Elections for March 22, 2026**
Slovenia will hold its next legislative elections on March 22, 2026, as announced by President Natasa Pirc Musar. The official call for elections will be signed by the President in early January, allowing the campaign to begin on January 12, according to public broadcaster RTV Slo. The current left-liberal coalition government, led by Prime Minister Robert Golob's Movement for Freedom (GS), the Social Democrats (SD), and the Left (Levica), took office after the 2022 elections and holds 53 of the 90 parliamentary seats. Recent polls indicate a decline in support for the ruling coalition, potentially paving the way for a return to power of former Prime Minister Janez Jansa, a right-wing populist, euro-skeptic, and close ally of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán. The Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS), led by Jansa, leads the polls with nearly 22% of vote intentions, more than five percentage points ahead of Golob's GS, which stands at 16.6%. The opposition coalition, including smaller parties, collectively holds around 33% of vote intentions, while the ruling coalition's combined support is estimated at 26% to 27%. Slovenia, a country in northwestern Balkans with over 2.1 million inhabitants, became independent from Yugoslavia in 1991 and is currently a member of NATO and the European Union.

--- Deep Research Analysis ---
Since January 2026, Slovenian politics has been dominated by the lead-up to the March 22, 2026 parliamentary elections, with both the ruling Freedom Movement (Gibanje Svoboda/GS) and the opposition Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS) facing distinct challenges, setbacks, and controversies.

**Freedom Movement (GS) Setbacks and Government Controversies**

The ruling Freedom Movement, led by Prime Minister Robert Golob, has experienced significant polling declines since January 2026. According to a February 1, 2026 survey by Ninamedia, the party trailed SDS with only 19.1% support compared to SDS's 22.6%, with a high number of undecided voters at 19.7% [14]. By mid-February, a Mediana survey for RTV Slovenija projected the Freedom Movement would secure only 23 seats—well below the 45 needed for a majority—while the entire center-left coalition (GS, Social Democrats, and Left) was projected at just 39 seats [11].

Several specific controversies have affected the Freedom Movement's campaign:

*Policy Debates and Economic Concerns:* The government has faced criticism over Premier Golob's proposed "Šutarjev zakon" (Šutar's Law), described as controversial, alongside debates over the preferential voting system and Minister Luka Mesec's push for a minimum net wage of 1,000 euros [15]. The government also approved a defense resolution increasing military spending to 2% of GDP by 2030, generating debate about fiscal priorities [15].

*Corruption and Governance Scrutiny:* The government has faced scrutiny regarding alleged misuse of state resources and its handling of corruption investigations [15]. Additionally, controversies emerged over the procurement of military equipment for Ukraine, suggesting internal tensions over foreign policy commitments [15].

*Coalition Fragmentation:* Internal political tensions within the coalition have been documented, including disagreements over appointments and the role of referendums [15]. The political landscape has further fragmented with the formation of new parties, such as "Prerod" (Renaissance) led by European Parliament member Vladimir Prebilič, potentially drawing support from the government side [15][16].

**SDS Position and Challenges**

While the SDS, led by longtime opposition figure Janez Janša, has maintained a lead in polls—projected at 30 seats in one February survey [11] or 22.5-22.6% in others [14][16]—the party faces distinct challenges from both its right flank and its own historical baggage.

*Populist Competition:* The emergence of the right-populist party Resni.ca (Truth), founded by anti-COVID measures activist Zoran Stevanović, has created significant pressure on SDS's right flank. Resni.ca campaigns on an anti-establishment, anti-EU platform with the slogan "Dem Volk die Macht" (Power to the People) and was projected to win four seats [11][13]. Stevanović had previously signed a 2021 document explicitly ruling out cooperation with SDS, raising concerns about potential political instability even if SDS wins the plurality [11][13].

*Janša's Controversial Rhetoric:* In January 2026, Janša generated controversy by referring to opposition parties as a "political para-military left" (political paravojska levice) and declaring that the first action after elections should be to "close the taps"—a metaphor suggesting termination of funding or support to opposition groups [18]. He also stated his party would no longer form minority or crisis governments and would instead aim for a constitutional majority [18].

*Historical Corruption Issues:* Media coverage has highlighted Janša's past legal troubles, including imprisonments for treason (1988) and corruption (2014), as well as his self-presentation as a fighter against a post-communist establishment [13]. While Janša has adopted hardline positions on migration modeled after Hungary's Viktor Orbán, his pro-Ukraine stance has made him vulnerable to attacks from populist rivals like Stevanović [11][13].

**Economic and Electoral Context**

The electoral environment reflects broader economic anxieties, with debates over military spending, wage policy, and state resource management featuring prominently. The fragmented polling landscape—showing SDS leading but potentially unable to form a stable coalition due to Resni.ca's spoiler effect, while the Freedom Movement faces significant losses—suggests Slovenia is heading toward a potential rightward shift, though the exact government formation remains uncertain given the complex coalition arithmetic and high number of undecided voters [11][12][13][14].
